debrief.features: The difference between the AI generated text and Human written content is readily apparent. Human writing tends to convey more feeling and emotional context, capturing the impact of a situation through nuanced language. In contrast, the AI generated text is more technical and objective, focusing on factual descriptions while conveying little, if any, emotions within the wording. As a result, AI writing often explains what happened and possible resolutions in a clinical manner, without reflecting the human experience or emotional weight of the situation.

debrief.features: Overuse of hyphens, em dashes, or commas. Formatting issues. Repeated points or sentences subsequently.
I look for consistent tone, structured explanations and lack of natural personal variation

Some of the features that I look for when identifying AI-generated text would include the text suspiciously containing pure monotone text that reads as spitting out pure factual information without considering the audience's engagement to the content. I also look for simple context mistakes that a human wouldn't normally make, such as randomly switching point of views throughout a passage, sentences that feel disconnected or irrelevant to the previous sentences,  lack of citation or overconfidently claiming credit for personal citations/credit, hallucinations, etc. It is also important for me to check for a passage that reads too strictly formal, with generic patterns an AI model usually makes, such as lacking human flavor/relatability ingrained in the text.

anonymization_note: Prolific identifiers (PID, studyId, sessionId) and browser fingerprints (IP, User-Agent, internal KV key) have been stripped. The random per-session UUID in each record's `sessionId` field is retained as the only per-response identifier. This field cannot be traced back to Prolific participant identity.
